sections.heading: Securities and Exchange Board of India ("SEBI"/ "the Board"), from time to time,
sections.title: Outsourcing policy framework for intermediaries
sections.summary: Intermediaries must establish a comprehensive outsourcing policy that defines which activities can be outsourced, who approves outsourcing decisions, and how third parties are selected. The policy must evaluate risks like supervisory impairment, risk concentrations, and acceptable outsourcing limits. The Board is responsible for policy implementation and regular reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a detailed outsourcing policy to assess which activities can be outsourced, relevant for DTIA when transferring data across borders with third parties.

sections.source_quote: An intermediary seeking to outsource activities shall have in place a comprehensive policy to guide the assessment of whether and how those activities can be appropriately outsourced. The Board / partners (as the case may be) {hereinafter referred to as the "the Board"} of the intermediary shall have the responsibility for the outsourcing policy and related overall responsibility for activities undertaken under that policy.

sections.heading: SEBI Master Circular No. SEBI/HO/MIRSD-PoD-2/P/CIR/2023/89 dated June 15,
sections.title: Outsourcing risk management program for intermediaries
sections.summary: Intermediaries must establish a comprehensive outsourcing risk management program to assess risks from outsourced activities. This includes evaluating the impact of third-party failures on financial, reputational, and operational performance, back-up arrangements, third-party regulatory status, and conflict of interest risks. The program must ensure arm's length relationships with third parties and maintain centralized records for review by the Board.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires risk assessment of outsourced activities, relevant for DTIA when transferring data across borders with third parties.

requirements.text: Establish comprehensive outsourcing risk management program
Assess outsourcing risk based on scope, materiality, and impact of third-party failure
Evaluate impact of third-party failure on financial, reputational, and operational performance
Evaluate ability to cope with non-performance through back-up arrangements
Assess regulatory status of third party including fitness and probity
Address potential conflicts of interest between intermediary and third party
Ensure arm's length distance between intermediary and third party in infrastructure, manpower, decision-making, record keeping
Maintain centralized records of outsourced activities for Board review
Review financial and operational capabilities of third party to assess continuity

sections.source_quote: The intermediary shall establish a comprehensive outsourcing risk management programme to address the outsourced activities and the relationship with the third party.

sections.title: Outsourcing liability and regulatory compliance
sections.summary: Intermediaries remain fully liable for outsourced activities as if performed in-house. Outsourcing arrangements must not affect investors' rights against the intermediary, and the intermediary is liable for losses due to third-party failures. Facilities and data involved in outsourced activities are deemed the intermediary's, and the intermediary must allow regulators and authorized persons to inspect them. Outsourcing must not impair regulators' ability to supervise the intermediary.

sections.title: Contractual requirements for outsourcing arrangements
sections.summary: Outsourcing relationships must be governed by written contracts that clearly define activities, responsibilities, confidentiality, termination procedures, and risk management. Contracts must include service levels, mutual obligations, liability for unsatisfactory performance, monitoring mechanisms, sub-contracting conditions, data security, and provisions for regulatory access.

sections.title: Contingency planning for intermediaries and third parties
sections.summary: Intermediaries and their third parties must establish and maintain contingency plans, including disaster recovery and periodic testing of backup facilities. Specific contingency plans must be developed for each outsourcing arrangement. Intermediaries must assess and address potential consequences of business disruptions or third-party failures, including coordination of contingency plans and provisions for third-party non-performance. Robust IT security is necessary to ensure business continuity and protect customer privacy and regulatory obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires intermediaries to implement and test disaster recovery plans for third parties to ensure business continuity and data security during cross-border or third-party data transfers.

sections.title: Referring SEBI for enforcement actions against RAs/IAs
sections.summary: This section authorizes SEBI to refer enforcement actions against Registered Advisers and Investment Advisors (RAs/IAs) to SEBI itself. It also permits recognized RAASB/IAASB bodies to conduct on-site or off-site inspections of RAs/IAs on behalf of SEBI, with any additional activities specified by SEBI.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows SEBI to initiate enforcement actions against RAs/IAs, which may involve cross-border data transfers if RAs/IAs operate internationally, requiring DTIA assessments for compliance with enforcement procedures.

sections.heading: Framework for Regulatory Sandbox
sections.title: Investment advisers cannot seek power of attorney from clients
sections.summary: This section mandates that investment advisers must explicitly state they will not request any power of attorney or authorizations from clients for implementing investment advice. The obligation applies to all investment advisers operating under the SEBI regulations. The requirement ensures clients retain control over their financial decisions without delegation of authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts the transfer of client authority through power of attorney, which could impact data handling if advisers use such mechanisms for client transactions.

requirements.text: Investment Adviser shall clearly declare it shall not seek any power of attorney or authorizations from clients for implementation of investment advice

sections.source_quote: 14. No right to seek Power of Attorney: The Investment Adviser to clearly declare that it shall not seek any power of attorney or authorizations from its clients for implementation of investment advice.

sections.title: Investment advisers must specify fee terms and payment methods
sections.summary: This section requires investment advisers to provide specific details on fee quantum, payment methods, periodicity, and billing terms. It mandates that fees be paid through traceable modes like NEFT/RTGS/IMPS/UPI or demand drafts, excluding cash payments. The obligation applies to all investment advisers and ensures transparent fee structures for client transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies payment methods and fee structures, which are relevant for financial data handling and cross-border transactions involving payment systems.

requirements.text: Provide specific details on quantum and manner of payment of fees for investment advice rendered
Fee modalities and periodicity must be specified by attaching a detailed fee schedule to the agreement
Illustration(s) on how the fee will be determined must be provided
Specify whether payment to be made in advance
Specify type of documents evidencing receipt of payment of fee
Specify periodicity of billing with clear date and service period
Payment of fees shall be through a mode which shows traceability of funds
Fees shall not be accepted in cash

sections.source_quote: 17. Terms of fees and billing: (a) Provide specific details on the following: i. The quantum and manner of payment of fees for investment advice rendered. ii. Fee modalities and periodicity, by attaching a detailed fee schedule to the agreement; iii. Illustration(s) on how the fee will be determined; iv. whether payment to be made in advance; v. type of documents evidencing receipt of payment of fee; vi. Periodicity of billing with clear date and service period (b) The payment of fees shall be through a mode which shows traceability of funds. Such modes may include account payee crossed cheque/Demand Drafts or by way of direct credit to the bank accounts through NEFT/RTGS/IMPS/UPI or any other mode specified by SEBI from time to time. However, the fees shall not be accepted in cash.

sections.heading: Know Your Client (KYC) norms for the securities market
sections.title: Investment advisers not liable for client asset devaluation
sections.summary: This section states that investment advisers shall not incur liability for client losses due to asset devaluation, market fluctuations, or underperformance of securities. The obligation applies to all investment advisers and limits their financial responsibility for market-related client losses.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies liability for client asset devaluation, which is relevant for assessing risk exposure in cross-border data transfers involving financial assets.

requirements.text: Investment Adviser shall not incur any liability by reason of any loss, which a client may suffer by reason of any depletion in the value of the assets under advice

sections.source_quote: 18. Liability of Investment Adviser: The agreement to clearly state that the Investment Adviser shall not incur any liability by reason of any loss, which a client may suffer by reason of any depletion in the value of the assets under advice, which may result by reason of fluctuation in asset value, or by reason of non-performance or underperformance of the securities/funds or any other market conditions.

sections.title: Principles for outsourcing by securities intermediaries
sections.summary: This section provides specific principles for outsourcing activities by securities intermediaries, including data security, confidentiality, and compliance with regulatory requirements. It requires intermediaries to ensure that outsourced activities do not compromise regulatory oversight. The obligations apply to intermediaries outsourcing their activities.

sections.title: Confidential information protection for third parties
sections.summary: Intermediaries must take steps to require third parties to protect confidential information of both the intermediary and its customers from intentional or inadvertent disclosure. Third parties must limit employee access to data on a 'need to know' basis and implement adequate checks and balances. Intermediaries must ensure third parties maintain data security safeguards when providing services to multiple entities.

sections.title: Risk management for third-party concentration
sections.summary: Intermediaries must ensure strong safeguards to prevent co-mingling of information, documents, records, and assets when third parties act as outsourcing agents for multiple intermediaries. This section addresses risks associated with concentrated outsourcing activities to limited third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses risks of data co-mingling when third parties handle data for multiple intermediaries, requiring safeguards for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Third parties and intermediaries must ensure strong safeguards to prevent co-mingling of information, documents, records, and assets

sections.source_quote: In instances, where the third party acts as an outsourcing agent for multiple intermediaries, it is the duty of the third party and the intermediary to ensure that strong safeguards are put in place so that there is no co-mingling of information /documents, records and assets.

sections.summary: This section mandates that Registered Advisers and Investment Advisors (RAs/IAs) must provide SEBI with annual compliance audit reports and periodic/ad-hoc reports detailing general information about RAs/IAs, customer complaints, client details, and other relevant data. These reports are required to ensure regulatory oversight of financial advisory activities and compliance with SEBI's standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires RAs/IAs to submit detailed operational and client data reports to SEBI, which impacts data transfer assessments by establishing reporting obligations that may involve cross-border data flows for compliance purposes.

sections.title: Amendments and termination implications
sections.summary: This section details the implications of amendments, termination, and assignment of investment advisory agreements. It requires the investment adviser to address fee set-offs, refunds, completion of ongoing investments, transition support obligations, and other related matters in detail when agreements are amended or terminated.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it specifies obligations for data handling during agreement termination, including transition support and data transfer protocols.

sections.summary: This section requires investment advisers to declare that they maintain independent and arm's-length relationships with related parties. It mandates disclosures of conflicts of interest to ensure transparency in relationships with related entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it requires clear declarations of arm's length relationships, which can impact data sharing and processing with related parties across borders.

requirements.text: Investment Adviser must declare independent and arm's length relationship with related parties
Investment Adviser must disclose conflicts of interest with related parties

sections.source_quote: The Investment Adviser to clearly declare that it is carrying on its activities independently, at an arms-length basis with its related parties. Disclosures of conflicts to be made.

sections.heading: Unauthenticated news circulated by SEBI Registered Market Intermediaries
sections.title: Investment advisers engaged in other activities
sections.summary: This section specifies requirements for investment advisers engaged in other activities. Individual advisers must ensure arm's-length relationships with other activities and prohibit distribution services to clients. Non-individual advisers must also prohibit distribution services and investment advisory services to clients through their groups.
sections.dtia_summary: This section is relevant for DTIAs as it defines restrictions on data sharing with related parties and other activities, impacting cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Investment Adviser (individual) must maintain arm's length relationship with other activities
Individual Investment Adviser must not provide distribution services to clients
Family members of individual Investment Adviser must not provide distribution services to clients
Individual Investment Adviser must not provide investment advisory services to clients receiving distribution services from other family members
Non-individual Investment Adviser must not provide distribution services to advisory clients
Non-individual Investment Adviser must not provide investment advisory services to distribution clients

sections.source_quote: The Investment Adviser (individual) to represent to the client that it maintains an arms-length relationship between its activities as an investment adviser and other activities and to covenant that this arm's length relationship shall be maintained throughout the tenure of advisory service; In case of Investment Adviser who are individuals: (a) to represent that they shall not provide any distribution services. (b) to represent that the family of an individual Investment Adviser shall not provide distribution services to the client advised by the individual Investment Adviser, for securities and investment products. (c) to represent that they shall not provide investment advisory services, for securities and investment products, to a client who is receiving distribution services from other family members; The Investment Adviser (non-individual); (a) to represent that they shall not provide any distribution services, for securities and investment products, either directly or through their group to an advisory client. (b) to represent that they shall not provide investment advisory services, for securities and investment products, either directly or through their group to the distribution client.

sections.heading: General Guidelines for dealing with Conflicts of Interest of intermediaries
sections.title: Investment advisers must disclose conflicts of interest
sections.summary: This section requires investment advisers to disclose all conflicts of interest as they arise and prohibits any direct or indirect benefit from clients' securities or investment products. The obligation applies to all investment advisers. This ensures transparency in financial relationships and prevents conflicts that could affect client interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates disclosure of conflicts of interest, which is critical for assessing whether data transfers involve potential conflicts that could compromise client data.

requirements.text: Investment Adviser shall disclose all conflicts of interest as and when they arise
Investment Adviser shall not derive any direct or indirect benefit out of the client's securities/investment products

sections.source_quote: 15. No conflict of interest: The Investment Adviser to clearly declare that it will disclose all conflicts of interest as and when they arise and not derive any direct or indirect benefit out of the client's securities/investment products.

sections.title: Conflict of interest management for intermediaries
sections.summary: Intermediaries must establish policies with senior management involvement to identify and manage conflicts of interest, create internal codes of conduct, and ensure communication to all staff. They must maintain integrity, provide fair treatment to clients, prioritize client interests, disclose potential conflicts, reduce conflicts through information barriers, restrict transactions to avoid conflicts, avoid using material non-public information, and ensure no incentive structures encourage unsuitable product sales. These obligations apply to intermediaries and their associated persons in securities markets.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires intermediaries to implement conflict of interest management systems that could impact cross-border data transfers involving client information, especially when conflicts arise between intermediaries and clients.

sections.summary: This section states that investment advisers shall not incur liability for client losses due to asset devaluation, market fluctuations, or underperformance of securities. sections.title: Outsourcing policy framework for intermediaries
sections.summary: Intermediaries must establish a comprehensive outsourcing policy that defines which activities can be outsourced, who approves outsourcing decisions, and how third parties are selected. The policy must evaluate risks like supervisory impairment, risk concentrations, and acceptable outsourcing limits. The Board is responsible for policy implementation and regular reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a detailed outsourcing policy to assess which activities can be outsourced, relevant for DTIA when transferring data across borders with third parties.

sections.title: Outsourcing risk management program for intermediaries
sections.summary: Intermediaries must establish a comprehensive outsourcing risk management program to assess risks from outsourced activities. This includes evaluating the impact of third-party failures on financial, reputational, and operational performance, back-up arrangements, third-party regulatory status, and conflict of interest risks. The program must ensure arm's length relationships with third parties and maintain centralized records for review by the Board.

sections.title: Outsourcing liability and regulatory compliance
sections.summary: Intermediaries remain fully liable for outsourced activities as if performed in-house. Outsourcing arrangements must not affect investors' rights against the intermediary, and the intermediary is liable for losses due to third-party failures. Facilities and data involved in outsourced activities are deemed the intermediary's, and the intermediary must allow regulators and authorized persons to inspect them. Outsourcing must not impair regulators' ability to supervise the intermediary.

sections.title: Contingency planning for intermediaries and third parties
sections.summary: Intermediaries and their third parties must establish and maintain contingency plans, including disaster recovery and periodic testing of backup facilities. Intermediaries must assess and address potential consequences of business disruptions or third-party failures, including coordination of contingency plans and provisions for third-party non-performance. Robust IT security is necessary to ensure business continuity and protect customer privacy and regulatory obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires intermediaries to implement and test disaster recovery plans for third parties to ensure business continuity and data security during cross-border or third-party data transfers.

requirements.text: Intermediaries and third parties must establish and maintain contingency plans including disaster recovery and periodic testing of backup facilities
Specific contingency plans must be separately developed for each outsourcing arrangement
Intermediaries must assess and address potential consequences of business disruptions or third-party failures
Intermediaries must coordinate contingency plans with third parties
Intermediaries must have contingency plans for third-party non-performance
Intermediaries must ensure third parties maintain robust IT security and disaster recovery capabilities
Intermediaries must periodically test critical security procedures and systems

sections.source_quote: The intermediary and its third parties shall establish and maintain contingency plans, including a plan for disaster recovery and periodic testing of backup facilities.

sections.heading: The intermediary shall take appropriate steps to require that third parties protect
sections.title: Confidential information protection for third parties
sections.summary: Intermediaries must take steps to require third parties to protect confidential information of both the intermediary and its customers from intentional or inadvertent disclosure. Third parties must limit employee access to data on a 'need to know' basis and implement adequate checks and balances. Intermediaries must ensure third parties maintain data security safeguards when providing services to multiple entities.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates that third parties handling financial data must implement strict confidentiality measures to prevent data breaches during cross-border or third-party data transfers.

requirements.text: Intermediaries must require third parties to protect confidential information from unauthorized disclosure
Third parties must limit employee access to data on a 'need to know' basis
Third parties must implement adequate checks and balances for data security
Intermediaries must ensure third parties maintain data security safeguards when serving multiple entities

sections.source_quote: The intermediary shall take appropriate steps to require that third parties protect confidential information of both the intermediary and its customers from intentional or inadvertent disclosure to unauthorised persons.

sections.heading: Potential risks posed where the outsourced activities of multiple intermediaries
sections.title: Risk management for third-party concentration
sections.summary: Intermediaries must ensure strong safeguards to prevent co-mingling of information, documents, records, and assets when third parties act as outsourcing agents for multiple intermediaries. This section addresses risks associated with concentrated outsourcing activities to limited third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses risks of data co-mingling when third parties handle data for multiple intermediaries, requiring safeguards for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Third parties and intermediaries must ensure strong safeguards to prevent co-mingling of information, documents, records, and assets

sections.source_quote: In instances, where the third party acts as an outsourcing agent for multiple intermediaries, it is the duty of the third party and the intermediary to ensure that strong safeguards are put in place so that there is no co-mingling of information /documents, records and assets.

sections.heading: Potential risks posed where the outsourced activities of multiple intermediaries (cont.)
